title: Gloucester City Council – HMO Master Schema
description: A single consolidated master schema containing all structured information for Gloucester City Council's HMO service. Consolidates: central government legislation and guidance, council strategy, local policy, operational guidance, and value/quality/consistency framework.

centralGovernmentLegislationAndGuidance.description: Central government legislation and guidance schema. Source: hmo_central_gov_schema_v2.json

schemaScope.const: Central government primary legislation, secondary legislation (statutory instruments) and national guidance only. Local authority policy, local plans and supplementary planning documents are excluded from this schema version.

hmoDefinitions.description: Statutory definitions of HMO types derived solely from primary and secondary central government legislation

description.const: A property occupied by 3 or more people forming 2 or more households who share amenities such as a kitchen, bathroom or toilet.

standardHMO.description: The standard HMO definition under the Housing Act 2004 – occupied as main or only residence by persons forming more than one household, sharing basic amenities

selfContainedFlatHMO.description: HMO where a self-contained flat is occupied by persons forming more than one household and the building was converted before 1991 regulations

section257HMO.description: A converted block of self-contained flats where the conversion did not comply with 1991 Building Regulations and fewer than two-thirds of the flats are owner-occupied

buildingRegulationsTest.const: Conversion did not comply with Building Regulations 1991 (or later)

description.const: Less than two-thirds of self-contained flats are owner-occupied

smallHMO_C4.description: Planning use class for small HMOs. Defined in planning law as 3 to 6 unrelated individuals sharing as their only or main residence.

permittedDevelopmentRight.description: Change of use from C3 dwelling house to C4 HMO is permitted development and does not normally require planning permission

legislativeSource.const: Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) (England) Order 2015, Schedule 2, Part 3, Class L
Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 (as amended)

largeHMO_SuiGeneris.description: Planning use for HMOs with 7 or more occupants. Classified as Sui Generis – outside any use class. Always requires planning permission for change of use.

mandatoryLicensingThreshold.description: Note: the mandatory licensing threshold under housing law is 5+ occupants, distinct from the 7+ planning threshold

legislativeSource.const: Licensing of Houses in Multiple Occupation (Prescribed Description) (England) Order 2018, SI 2018/221
Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 (as amended); not within any prescribed use class

householdDefinition.description: Statutory definition of 'household' for HMO purposes

description.const: A household consists of: a single person; or members of the same family living together (including couples, parents and children, grandparents, siblings, and half-relatives). Persons living together as a couple are treated as members of the same household.

statutoryExemptions.description: Categories of property or occupation that are NOT classed as HMOs under Schedule 14 of the Housing Act 2004

default.description: Property where owner-occupier lives with no more than two lodgers
Buildings managed or owned by a local housing authority, registered social landlord, police authority, fire and rescue authority, or health service body
Student halls of residence managed or controlled by a specified educational establishment
Buildings occupied principally by members of a religious community whose principal occupation is prayer, contemplation, education or the relief of suffering

default.category: Purpose-built block of flats (mandatory licensing exemption)
default.description: Purpose-built flat in a block of 3 or more self-contained flats is excluded from mandatory licensing (though may fall within local additional licensing schemes)
default.legislativeSource: Licensing of Houses in Multiple Occupation (Prescribed Description) (England) Order 2018, SI 2018/221, article 3(2)

default.category: Buildings with two or fewer occupants
default.description: A property occupied by only two persons does not meet the minimum occupancy threshold to constitute an HMO

primaryLegislation.description: Primary Acts of Parliament enacted by the UK Parliament governing HMOs in England

default.title: Housing Act 2004
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: The primary enabling Act for all HMO regulation in England. Establishes the HHSRS, mandatory HMO licensing, additional and selective licensing, management standards, enforcement powers and the statutory definition of HMO.

keyParts.title: Housing Conditions
keyParts.description: Introduces the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS). Replaces the fitness standard with a risk-based framework assessing 29 prescribed hazard categories. Establishes Category 1 (serious) and Category 2 hazards. Mandates council enforcement action for Category 1 hazards.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Councils are legally obliged to take enforcement action where a Category 1 hazard is identified in an HMO. Provides powers for Improvement Notices, Prohibition Orders, Hazard Awareness Notices and Emergency Remedial Action.

keyParts.title: Licensing of Houses in Multiple Occupation
keyParts.description: Establishes mandatory HMO licensing for properties meeting the prescribed description. Sets out the licence application process, fit and proper person test, licence conditions, duration, variation, revocation and appeal rights. Creates the offence of operating an unlicensed HMO.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Core licensing framework. Mandatory licence required for HMOs meeting the 2018 prescribed description (5+ occupants, 2+ households). Unlimited fine on conviction for unlicensed operation.

keyParts.title: Additional Licensing
keyParts.description: Empowers local housing authorities to designate additional licensing schemes covering HMOs not subject to mandatory licensing. Schemes must satisfy a statutory reasonableness and desirability test and require MHCLG approval if covering more than 20% of private rented stock.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Enables councils to extend licensing to smaller HMOs (3–4 occupants) not captured by mandatory scheme.

keyParts.title: Selective Licensing of Other Residential Accommodation
keyParts.description: Enables councils to require licences for all privately rented properties in designated areas experiencing low housing demand, significant anti-social behaviour or poor property conditions.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Can apply to all rented dwellings in an area, including HMOs, regardless of occupant numbers.

keyParts.title: Management Orders
keyParts.description: Provides for Interim and Final Management Orders allowing councils to take over management of a property where an HMO is unlicensed, or licensable conditions cannot be met, or serious management failures exist.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Backstop enforcement power applicable to persistently non-compliant HMOs.

keyParts.title: Supplementary Provisions
keyParts.description: Includes tenancy deposit protection provisions and powers to share information between enforcement authorities.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Tenancy deposit requirements apply to HMO tenancies.

keyParts.title: Licence Application and Decision
keyParts.description: Sets out the procedure for granting licences, including the duty to consult and notification requirements.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Procedural requirements for all HMO licence applications.

keyParts.title: Fit and Proper Person Test
keyParts.description: Sets out criteria for determining whether a proposed licence holder or manager is fit and proper, including criminal convictions, discrimination findings and breaches of housing law.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Every HMO licence applicant must satisfy this test.

keyParts.title: Offences in relation to licensing of HMOs
keyParts.description: Creates the criminal offence of operating an unlicensed HMO and the offence of failing to comply with licence conditions. As amended by the Renters' Rights Act 2025, civil penalties may also be imposed.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Primary enforcement provision; unlimited fine on conviction.

keyParts.title: Management Regulations – Offence of Non-Compliance
keyParts.description: Creates an offence for failure to comply with HMO Management Regulations. Each failure constitutes a separate offence.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Basis for civil penalties or prosecution where management regulation duties are breached.

keyParts.title: Definition of HMO
keyParts.description: Provides the statutory tests for determining whether a property is an HMO: the standard test (s.254(2)), the self-contained flat test (s.254(3)), the converted building test (s.254(4)), and the section 257 converted block test.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Foundation of the entire regulatory framework.

keyParts.title: Definition of Household
keyParts.description: Defines 'household' for HMO purposes.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Essential for determining whether 2+ household threshold is met.

keyParts.title: Person Having Control / Person Managing
keyParts.description: Defines 'person having control' (entitled to receive rack rent) and 'person managing' (receives rent on behalf of another) for enforcement purposes.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Determines who is legally responsible for HMO compliance and enforcement liability.

keyParts.title: Exemptions from HMO Definition
keyParts.description: Lists categories of building or occupation that are not classed as HMOs for any purpose under the Act.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Defines the boundaries of the licensing and management regime.

default.title: Housing Act 1985
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Precursor consolidating legislation. Section 345 contained the original statutory HMO definition derived from the Housing Act 1974. Largely superseded for HMO purposes by the Housing Act 2004.

keyParts.title: Original HMO Definition
keyParts.description: First consolidated statutory definition of a house in multiple occupation.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Historical basis of modern HMO law; relevant where pre-2004 legal questions arise.

default.title: Housing and Planning Act 2016
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Significantly strengthened the enforcement toolkit available to local housing authorities dealing with non-compliant HMO landlords.

keyParts.title: Civil Penalties
keyParts.description: Enables local housing authorities to impose civil penalties of up to £30,000 as an alternative to prosecution for housing offences including unlicensed HMO operation, breach of licence conditions and breach of management regulations.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Primary civil enforcement mechanism. Applies per offence; multiple breaches attract separate penalties.

keyParts.title: Rent Repayment Orders
keyParts.description: Enables tenants (and local authorities) to apply to the First-tier Tribunal for a Rent Repayment Order requiring a landlord to repay up to 12 months' rent where specified offences are committed. Tenants may apply directly without waiting for criminal conviction.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Applicable where HMO operates without a licence. Significant financial deterrent.

keyParts.title: Banning Orders
keyParts.description: Empowers courts to make banning orders preventing a person from letting or managing residential property, following conviction for a banning order offence.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Applicable to rogue HMO landlords convicted of relevant offences.

keyParts.title: Database of Rogue Landlords and Property Agents
keyParts.description: Establishes a national database (accessible to local authorities) recording banning orders and persons convicted of relevant offences.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Relevant to fit and proper person assessments for HMO licence applications.

default.title: Renters' Rights Act 2025
default.commencementNotes: Core tenancy reforms commenced 1 May 2026. Enforcement and investigation powers commenced 27 December 2025.
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Wide-ranging reform of the private rented sector including HMOs. Abolishes fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies, introduces PRS database, extends the Decent Homes Standard and Awaab's Law to the private sector, and expands civil penalty powers for HMO licensing offences.

keyParts.title: Abolition of Fixed-Term Assured Shorthold Tenancies
keyParts.description: Abolishes the assured shorthold tenancy. All existing assured shorthold tenancies became assured periodic tenancies on 1 May 2026. Landlords prohibited from creating new fixed-term tenancies.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Affects all HMO tenancy agreements. Landlords cannot lock tenants into fixed terms.

keyParts.title: Abolition of Section 21 No-Fault Eviction
keyParts.description: Removes the Section 21 Housing Act 1988 no-fault eviction process. Landlords may only recover possession on specified grounds under Schedule 2 of the Housing Act 1988 (as amended).
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords can no longer use Section 21 to recover possession. Must use statutory grounds.

keyParts.title: Private Rented Sector (PRS) Database
keyParts.description: Establishes a mandatory national database requiring landlords to register themselves and their properties. Tenants can access information about their landlord. Local authorities can use the database for enforcement targeting.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords and managers must register on the PRS Database. Non-registration is a civil penalty offence.

keyParts.title: Landlord Ombudsman
keyParts.description: Establishes a mandatory Landlord Ombudsman scheme. All private landlords must be members. Tenants can bring complaints about management.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords required to join. Relevant to dispute resolution with HMO tenants.

keyParts.title: Decent Homes Standard – Extension to Private Rented Sector
keyParts.description: Extends the Decent Homes Standard (previously applicable to social housing only) to private rented properties, including HMOs.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMOs must meet the Decent Homes Standard once implementing regulations are in force.

keyParts.title: Awaab's Law – Private Rented Sector
keyParts.description: Extends Awaab's Law (mandatory response timescales for damp and mould hazards) to the private rented sector.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords must investigate and remediate damp and mould within prescribed timescales.

keyParts.title: Amendment of Housing Act 2004 – Expanded Civil Penalty Powers
keyParts.description: Amends sections 72 and 95 of the Housing Act 2004 to extend the persons against whom civil penalties may be imposed for HMO licensing offences, including superior landlords.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Widens enforcement net for unlicensed HMO operations.

keyParts.title: Rent Increases
keyParts.description: Limits rent increases to once per 12-month period. Abolishes rent review clauses in tenancy agreements.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Applies to all HMO periodic tenancies.

keyParts.title: Prohibitions on Discrimination
keyParts.description: Makes it unlawful for landlords and letting agents to refuse to let to tenants with children or those in receipt of benefits.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords and agents must not discriminate on these grounds.

keyParts.title: Local Authority Enforcement Powers
keyParts.description: Grants local authorities new statutory powers to enter business and residential premises to investigate potential offences (with and without warrant). Commenced 27 December 2025.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Strengthens local authority ability to inspect and investigate unlicensed or non-compliant HMOs.

default.title: Tenant Fees Act 2019
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Prohibits most fees charged to tenants by landlords and letting agents. Applies to HMO tenancies in England.

keyParts.title: Prohibited Payments
keyParts.description: Prohibits charging fees to tenants other than: rent, refundable tenancy deposit (capped at 5 weeks' rent for annual rent under £50,000), refundable holding deposit (capped at 1 week's rent), payments in default, and certain permitted payments (change of sharer, early termination where requested by tenant).
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords and agents must not charge prohibited fees. Breach attracts civil penalty up to £5,000 (first offence) or criminal prosecution (repeat offence).

default.title: Town and Country Planning Act 1990
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Principal planning Act establishing the development control framework, use classes system, and planning enforcement powers. Provides the enabling basis for permitted development rights and Article 4 Directions.

keyParts.title: Definition of Development
keyParts.description: Defines 'development' including material change of use of land or buildings. Change of use from C3 to Sui Generis (large HMO) constitutes development requiring planning permission.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Change of use to large HMO (7+ occupants) requires planning permission as a material change of use.

keyParts.title: Development Orders – Permitted Development
keyParts.description: Enables the Secretary of State to make development orders granting blanket planning permission for specified classes of development (including C3 to C4 conversion).
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Enabling provision for the GPDO 2015 permitted development right for small HMOs.

keyParts.title: Article 4 Directions
keyParts.description: Empowers local planning authorities to make Article 4 Directions to withdraw specified permitted development rights in defined geographic areas.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Councils may use Article 4 Directions to require planning permission for C3 to C4 HMO conversions in areas where they wish to manage HMO concentration.

keyParts.title: Planning Enforcement
keyParts.description: Provides enforcement notice, stop notice, injunction, and breach of condition notice powers where development is undertaken without required permission.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Enables councils to enforce against unauthorised large HMO use or breaches of planning conditions.

default.title: Building Act 1984
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Provides the statutory framework for Building Regulations. Compliance with Building Regulations is required for conversion and structural works in HMOs, and is relevant to the section 257 HMO definition.

keyParts.title: Power to make Building Regulations
keyParts.description: Enables the Secretary of State to make Building Regulations prescribing standards for building construction and conversion.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Building Regulations approval required for conversion works creating or modifying HMOs. The 1991 Building Regulations compliance test underpins the section 257 HMO category.

default.title: Environmental Protection Act 1990
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Statutory nuisance and waste enforcement provisions applicable to HMOs generating noise, pest, or waste-related nuisances.

keyParts.title: Statutory Nuisances
keyParts.description: Defines statutory nuisances including premises in a state prejudicial to health, smoke, fumes, noise and accumulations of waste. Councils must serve abatement notices where a statutory nuisance exists or is likely to occur.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: HMOs generating noise, pest infestation, waste accumulation or damp conditions prejudicial to health may be subject to statutory nuisance abatement notices.

default.title: Fire Safety Act 2021
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Clarifies the scope of fire safety duties under the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, particularly for multi-occupied residential buildings.

keyParts.title: Application to Buildings with Common Parts
keyParts.description: Clarifies that the RRO 2005 applies to the structure, external walls (including cladding and balconies), and flat entrance doors of multi-occupied residential buildings.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Confirms that fire risk assessments for multi-storey HMOs must cover external walls and flat entrance doors, not just common parts.

default.title: Energy Act 2011
default.overallRelevanceToHMO: Enabling Act for the Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards (MEES) regulations applicable to privately rented properties including HMOs.

keyParts.title: Energy Efficiency Regulations – Private Rented Sector
keyParts.description: Enables the Secretary of State to make regulations requiring minimum energy efficiency standards for domestic and non-domestic rented property.
keyParts.hmoRelevance: Basis for SI 2015/1825 (MEES Regulations) requiring HMO lettings to meet minimum EPC standard.

secondaryLegislation.description: Statutory Instruments made under primary Acts, directly governing HMOs in England

default.title: Licensing of Houses in Multiple Occupation (Prescribed Description) (England) Order 2018
keyRequirements: Mandatory licence required for any HMO occupied by 5 or more persons forming more than 1 household who share amenities
Removes the previous requirement that the building must have 3 or more storeys
Excludes purpose-built flats in a block of 3 or more self-contained flats from mandatory licensing
Councils remain obliged to licence all HMOs meeting the prescribed description within their area
default.hmoRelevance: Defines the precise threshold for mandatory HMO licensing. Most significant post-2004 change to the licensing framework.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Unlimited fine on conviction under Housing Act 2004, s.72; civil penalty up to £30,000

default.title: Management of Houses in Multiple Occupation (England) Regulations 2006
keyRequirements: Manager must display name, address and contact number in a prominent position within the HMO
Manager must provide contact details to occupiers on request
Fire escape routes must be kept free of obstruction; fire precautions maintained and tested
Shared amenities (kitchens, bathrooms, toilets) kept clean, in good repair and safe
Common parts (hallways, stairs, landings, lobbies, porches) kept clean, in good repair and adequately lit
Adequate and suitable waste storage provided; refuse collection requirements facilitated
Gardens and shared outdoor areas kept in safe condition
Structural elements kept in good repair and working order
Manager must supply contact information to the local housing authority on request
default.hmoRelevance: Sets the day-to-day management standard for all HMOs with shared facilities. Applies regardless of whether the HMO is licensable.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Each failure constitutes a separate offence – civil penalty up to £30,000 per offence or unlimited fine on prosecution (Housing Act 2004, s.234)

default.title: Licensing and Management of Houses in Multiple Occupation and Other Houses (Miscellaneous Provisions) (England) Regulations 2006
keyRequirements: Prescribed content of HMO licence applications (identity of applicant, managing agent, mortgage lender; declaration of fit and proper status)
Prescribed period for determining licence applications
Requirements for interim licences
Overcrowding notices – minimum room sizes below which a room must not be used as sleeping accommodation: 6.51 sqm (1 adult), 10.22 sqm (2 adults), 4.64 sqm (child aged 10 or over)
default.hmoRelevance: Sets the procedural framework for licence applications and prescribes minimum sleeping room sizes enforceable via overcrowding notices.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Overcrowding contrary to notice: unlimited fine on prosecution; civil penalty up to £30,000

default.title: Houses in Multiple Occupation (Specified Activities) (England) Regulations 2016
keyRequirements: Specifies activities which constitute being involved in the management of a HMO for the purposes of the fit and proper person test
Relevant activities include: collecting rent, arranging or conducting viewings, arranging repairs, managing tenancies
default.hmoRelevance: Defines the activities that bring a managing agent within scope of the fit and proper person assessment.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Not directly a penalty provision – relevant to licence eligibility

default.title: Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 (as amended)
default.enablingAct: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, section 55(2)(f)
default.lastSignificantAmendment: Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) (Amendment) (England) Regulations 2020
keyRequirements: Class C3: Dwelling houses – single household occupation
Class C4: Houses in multiple occupation – 3 to 6 residents forming more than one household sharing amenities
Sui Generis: Large HMOs with 7 or more residents fall outside any use class; every material change of use requires planning permission
default.hmoRelevance: Defines the planning use class framework within which HMO conversions are assessed.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Breach of planning control – enforcement notice, stop notice, injunction under TCPA 1990

default.title: Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) (England) Order 2015 (as amended)
default.enablingAct: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, section 59
keyRequirements: Schedule 2, Part 3, Class L: Grants permitted development right for change of use from C3 (dwelling house) to C4 (small HMO) without planning permission
The right is removed where a local planning authority has made an Article 4 Direction removing Class L rights in a specific area
No permitted development right exists for change of use from C3 or C4 to Sui Generis large HMO – planning permission always required
default.hmoRelevance: Governs when planning permission is or is not required for HMO conversions. Central to understanding Article 4 Direction implications.
default.penaltiesForBreach: N/A – SI grants rights; breach of planning control where no permission held is addressed under TCPA 1990 enforcement

default.title: Housing Health and Safety Rating System (England) Regulations 2005
keyRequirements: Prescribes 29 categories of hazard to be assessed: includes damp and mould growth, excess cold, excess heat, asbestos, biocides, carbon monoxide, fire, falls, crowding and space, entry by intruders, noise, domestic hygiene and sanitation, structural collapse
Defines the method for calculating hazard scores to determine Category 1 (serious) and Category 2 (other) classifications
Local authority must take enforcement action for Category 1 hazards
Local authority has discretionary powers for Category 2 hazards
default.hmoRelevance: The operational basis for all HHSRS inspections in HMOs. Determines whether enforcement action is mandatory or discretionary.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Failure to comply with Improvement Notice: civil penalty up to £30,000 or unlimited fine on prosecution (Housing Act 2004, ss.30, 72)

default.title: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005
default.lastSignificantAmendment: Fire Safety Act 2021; Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022 (SI 2022/547)
keyRequirements: Responsible person must carry out a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment of common parts of an HMO
Assessment must identify significant fire risks and implement appropriate preventive and protective measures
Responsible person must ensure escape routes are kept clear and fire detection/suppression systems are maintained
For HMOs above 11 metres: additional requirements under SI 2022/547 including sharing safety information with residents and fire service
default.hmoRelevance: Applies to the common parts of all HMOs. Licensable HMOs require a formal documented fire risk assessment. Primary fire safety enforcement is by the Fire and Rescue Service.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Unlimited fine and/or up to 2 years' imprisonment for serious breaches; improvement, prohibition and enforcement notices

default.title: Smoke and Carbon Monoxide Alarm (Amendment) Regulations 2022
default.enablingAct: Housing Act 2004, section 150A (inserted by Deregulation Act 2015)
keyRequirements: At least one smoke alarm on every storey of the property that is used as living accommodation
At least one carbon monoxide alarm in any room used as living accommodation which contains a fixed combustion appliance (excluding gas cookers)
Alarms must be in working order at the start of each new tenancy
Landlord must repair or replace a faulty alarm as soon as reasonably practicable when notified by a tenant
Applies to all private rented dwellings, including all HMOs
default.hmoRelevance: Mandatory requirement for all HMO landlords. Each storey of an HMO requires a smoke alarm. Non-compliance – council may impose civil penalty up to £5,000.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Civil penalty up to £5,000 per breach

default.title: Electrical Safety Standards in the Private Rented Sector (England) Regulations 2020
keyRequirements: Electrical installations must be inspected and tested by a qualified person at least every 5 years
Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR) must satisfy BS 7671 (18th Edition IET Wiring Regulations)
EICR must be provided to new tenants before they occupy the property; to existing tenants within 28 days of request
EICR must be supplied to the local housing authority within 7 days of request
Remedial works identified in the EICR must be completed within 28 days (or shorter if the report specifies)
Evidence of remedial works must be provided to tenants and the local authority
default.hmoRelevance: Directly applies to all HMO landlords. Failure to comply allows the council to arrange remedial works and recover costs, and to impose civil penalties.

default.title: Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015 – Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards
keyRequirements: Privately rented dwellings must achieve a minimum EPC rating of E before they can be lawfully let
Landlords must obtain an EPC for each separately let unit in an HMO
Where a valid EPC is not available, the landlord must commission one

Exemptions available in limited circumstances (listed building consent refused; devaluation of property; recently installed maximum permitted improvement); exemptions must be registered on the PRS Exemptions Register
Government consultation ongoing (as of May 2026) on raising the minimum standard to EPC C by 2030
default.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords must hold valid EPCs and comply with the minimum E rating. Properties rated F or G cannot lawfully be let.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Civil penalty: up to £5,000 for lettings of less than 3 months; up to £4,000 for lettings of 3 months or more; up to £2,000 for failure to comply with compliance notice; up to £1,000 for providing false/misleading information to the register

default.title: Gas Safety (Installation and Use) Regulations 1998
default.enablingAct: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
keyRequirements: Landlords must ensure all gas appliances, fittings and flues are maintained in a safe condition
Annual gas safety check by a Gas Safe registered engineer required for each gas appliance and flue
Gas Safety Certificate (CP12) must be provided to existing tenants within 28 days of the check; to new tenants before occupation
Gas Safety Certificate must be retained for at least 2 years
default.hmoRelevance: Applies to all HMOs with gas installations. Annual CP12 certificate is a standard mandatory HMO licence condition.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Criminal offence – unlimited fine and/or up to 6 months' imprisonment

default.title: Council Tax (Liability for Owners) Regulations 1992
default.lastSignificantAmendment: Council Tax reform for HMO aggregation – guidance updated October 2024
keyRequirements: The owner (not the occupants) is the liable person for Council Tax in respect of properties constituting HMOs as defined in these Regulations
The Council Tax definition of HMO differs from the Housing Act 2004 definition
Aggregation changes (December 2023 guidance): licensed or unlicensed HMOs not currently aggregated must submit a proposal for amended banding
default.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords are liable for Council Tax, not individual tenants. Important for financial modelling of HMO investment.
default.penaltiesForBreach: Council Tax debt recovery enforcement by the billing authority

centralGovernmentGuidance.description: Statutory and non-statutory guidance published by central government departments (MHCLG, HSE, DLUHC, DCLG) relevant to HMO regulation. Excludes local authority guidance.

mustHaveRegard.description: Whether local authorities are under a legal duty to have regard to this guidance

default.title: Licensing of Houses in Multiple Occupation in England: A Guide for Landlords and Managers
default.publishedBy: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG)
default.summary: Statutory guidance explaining mandatory HMO licensing following the October 2018 changes. Covers the prescribed description, exemptions, licence conditions, fit and proper person test, overcrowding standards, and enforcement.
default.hmoRelevance: Local housing authorities must have regard to this guidance when operating mandatory HMO licensing schemes. Sets the national baseline for licence conditions and standards.

default.title: Houses in Multiple Occupation and Selective Licensing: Guidance for Local Housing Authorities
default.summary: Sets out the statutory test local authorities must satisfy to designate additional HMO licensing or selective licensing schemes, including evidence requirements for establishing that a scheme is 'reasonably necessary' and meets the desirability conditions. Schemes covering more than 20% of private rented sector dwellings or where the same type of scheme already covers more than 20% of the area require MHCLG confirmation.
default.hmoRelevance: Governs the legal conditions for implementing an additional licensing scheme. Councils must satisfy this guidance before designation.

default.title: Housing Health and Safety Rating System: Guidance for Landlords and Property Related Professionals
default.publishedBy: Department for Communities and Local Government (DCLG)
default.summary: Operational guidance on applying the HHSRS. Explains each of the 29 hazard categories, the scoring and banding methodology, when enforcement action is mandatory or discretionary, the range of enforcement powers available, and the appeals process.
default.hmoRelevance: Primary operational guidance for HHSRS inspections. Councils must follow this framework when assessing and enforcing conditions in HMOs.

default.title: Civil Penalties under the Renters' Rights Act 2025 and Other Housing Legislation
default.relatedLegislation: Renters' Rights Act 2025, s.104; Housing and Planning Act 2016, ss.28–31; Housing Act 2004
default.summary: Guidance on the civil penalty regime applicable to landlord offences including unlicensed HMO operation, breach of licence conditions, breach of management regulations, and failure to comply with improvement notices. Sets out relevant factors for determining penalty amounts and the process for imposing and appealing penalties.
default.hmoRelevance: Local authorities must have regard to this guidance when imposing civil penalties for HMO offences. Sets the national framework for enforcement decisions.

default.title: Nationally Described Space Standard
default.publishedBy: Department for Communities and Local Government (DCLG)
default.relatedLegislation: Town and Country Planning Act 1990 (adopted via local plan policies)
default.summary: Prescribes minimum gross internal floor areas and storage requirements for new dwellings. Not directly mandatory – adopted by local planning authorities through their local plans. Minimum bedroom sizes: 7.5 sqm (1 person, 7 years+); 11.5 sqm (2 persons, 7 years+). Overall dwelling minimum floor areas also prescribed by number of bedspaces.
default.hmoRelevance: Widely adopted by local authorities as the benchmark for HMO bedroom and unit sizes in planning applications for large HMOs.

default.title: Electrical Safety Standards in the Private Rented Sector – Guidance for Landlords, Tenants and Local Authorities
default.relatedLegislation: Electrical Safety Standards in the Private Rented Sector (England) Regulations 2020, SI 2020/312
default.summary: Explains landlord obligations to commission 5-yearly EICRs, supply them to tenants and councils, and complete remedial works within 28 days. Explains the local authority duty to enforce and the civil penalty regime.
default.hmoRelevance: Directly applicable to all HMO landlords. Councils must have regard when deciding whether and how to enforce EICR requirements.

default.title: Smoke and Carbon Monoxide Alarm (Amendment) Regulations 2022: Explanatory Booklet for Landlords
default.relatedLegislation: Smoke and Carbon Monoxide Alarm (Amendment) Regulations 2022, SI 2022/707
default.summary: Explains the requirements for smoke alarms on every storey of living accommodation and carbon monoxide alarms in rooms with fixed combustion appliances (excluding gas cookers). Covers testing, repair obligations and enforcement.
default.hmoRelevance: Directly applicable to all HMOs. Councils must enforce compliance and may issue civil penalties up to £5,000.

default.title: Energy Performance of Buildings: EPC Requirements and Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards
default.relatedLegislation: Energy Efficiency (Private Rented Property) (England and Wales) Regulations 2015, SI 2015/1825
default.summary: Explains the MEES requirement for minimum EPC rating of E for all private rented dwellings. Covers exemptions, the exemptions register, enforcement and proposed trajectory toward EPC C by 2030.
default.hmoRelevance: HMO landlords must hold EPCs for let units and comply with the minimum E rating. Councils may enforce and impose civil penalties. Proposed EPC C uplift (2030) will require HMO improvement programmes.

default.title: Housing – Fire Safety: Guidance on Fire Safety Provisions for Certain Types of Existing Housing (LACORS Guidance)
default.publishedBy: Local Authorities Coordinators of Regulatory Services (LACORS)
default.relatedLegislation: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005; Housing Act 2004
default.summary: Risk-based guidance providing a structured approach to fire safety assessment and precaution requirements in houses, HMOs, flats and other residential properties. Sets out appropriate fire detection grades, alarm categories, escape route standards and fire door requirements by property type and risk category.
default.hmoRelevance: The principal operational guidance for fire safety in HMOs used by local housing authorities and fire services. Widely referenced in HMO licence conditions as the standard to be achieved.

default.title: Private Rented Sector Database: Guidance for Landlords
default.summary: Guidance on the mandatory PRS Database requiring all private landlords to register themselves and their rental properties. Sets out registration requirements, what information must be provided, tenant access rights and enforcement.
default.hmoRelevance: All HMO landlords must register on the PRS Database. Failure to register is a civil penalty offence. Registration number is required on tenancy agreements.

default.title: Decent Homes Standard: Technical Guidance
default.summary: Defines the Decent Homes Standard: a property must meet the current statutory minimum standard (no Category 1 HHSRS hazards); be in a reasonable state of repair; have reasonably modern facilities and services; and provide a reasonable degree of thermal comfort.
default.hmoRelevance: Extension to the private rented sector via the Renters' Rights Act 2025 means HMOs will be required to meet the Decent Homes Standard once implementing regulations come into force.

default.title: Awaab's Law: Guidance on Hazard Repair Timescales
default.summary: Guidance on mandatory response timescales for damp and mould hazards in rented properties: landlords must investigate within a prescribed period and begin remediation within a further prescribed period. Implementing regulations will set the precise timescales for the PRS.
default.hmoRelevance: Will apply to HMO landlords once PRS implementing regulations are made. Damp and mould – a common HMO hazard – will trigger mandatory investigation and remediation timescales.

default.title: Approved Document B – Fire Safety (Volume 1: Dwellings)
default.publishedBy: HM Government / Building Regulations Advisory Committee
default.summary: Sets out methods of meeting Building Regulations requirements for fire safety in dwellings, including means of escape, internal fire spread (linings and structure), external fire spread and access for fire services. Volume 1 applies to dwellinghouses including those converted for HMO use.
default.hmoRelevance: Relevant to Building Regulations compliance for conversion works creating or altering HMOs. Applies alongside LACORS fire safety guidance.

default.title: Planning Policy Guidance: Housing
default.relatedLegislation: Town and Country Planning Act 1990; National Planning Policy Framework
default.summary: National Planning Practice Guidance (NPPG) on housing matters including housing mix, conversions and changes of use. Explains how local planning authorities should use evidence to manage housing mix and the use of Article 4 Directions.
default.hmoRelevance: Provides national guidance on managing the balance of housing types and the conditions under which Article 4 Directions removing C3 to C4 permitted development rights may be made.

default.title: National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF)
default.relatedLegislation: Planning and Compulsory Purchase Act 2004, s.19; Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023
default.summary: The overarching national planning policy framework. Sets out the government's planning policies and how these should be applied. The 2024 version strengthens the housing delivery duty and confirms that local plans and decisions must be consistent with the NPPF.
default.hmoRelevance: Local planning authorities, including Gloucester City Council, must ensure their planning policies for HMOs are consistent with the NPPF. Relevant to housing mix, design quality and sustainable development objectives.

objectiveTitle.const: Ensure that all our residents have somewhere to call home by providing more affordable housing, working closely with private and registered social landlords and providing improved support and accommodation to tackle homelessness.

objectiveNarrative.const: Provision of suitable, affordable, energy efficient housing is one of the UK's biggest challenges and, like many local authorities, we are focused on finding solutions that help local people. We will continue our important work safeguarding households threatened with homelessness and supporting those who find themselves without accommodation, while developing a new long-term strategy that brings together our plans to work with stakeholders to ensure all our residents have suitable housing. We will work with registered social landlords and developers to progress delivery of new housing schemes across the city and pursue other initiatives, such as converting vacant or underutilised properties and using the powers available to us to increase housing supply.

working closely with private and registered social landlords
We will work with registered social landlords and developers to progress delivery of new housing schemes across the city
work with stakeholders to ensure all our residents have suitable housing
Work with property owners to bring empty homes back into use and review the landlord incentive scheme to increase the number of private rented homes available.

allActionsInObjective.description: All verbatim actions listed under this objective in the Corporate Plan
default: Explore through the emerging Strategic and Local Plan, the possibility of increasing the requirement for affordable housing on all new major developments in the city from 20% to 30%.
Work with partners to support neighbourhood regeneration across the city, including the regeneration of Matson and Podsmead.
Deliver new council homes and invest in existing council homes, ensuring high energy efficiency standards to reduce running costs and emissions.
Explore options for the council to deliver social housing, including a social housing development company.
Secure more funding to tackle homelessness to ensure that good quality support and accommodation is available for those in need.
Work with property owners to bring empty homes back into use and review the landlord incentive scheme to increase the number of private rented homes available.
Introduce stronger mechanisms and regulatory powers, such as an Article 4 Direction, to control the proliferation of Houses in Multiple Occupation and improve standards in the private rented sector.

verbatimText.const: A HMO is a house in multiple occupation and applies to buildings that are either a single dwelling or a flat where one or more separate households share facilities such as; kitchens, toilet and bathroom.

policyTitle.const: Policy A2: Houses in Multiple Occupation

verbatimText.const: Planning permission for the creation of a House in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) will be permitted where: 1. The development would not result in any existing residential property (C3 use) being 'sandwiched' between two HMOs; and 2. The development would not result in the creation of more than two adjacent properties in HMO use; and 3. HMOs, including the proposed development, would represent no more than 10% of properties within a 100-metre radius of the application property.

policyA1.description: Policy A1 as cited in the GCC HMO guide for applicants

policyTitle.const: Policy A1: Effective and efficient use of housing, land and buildings

verbatimText.const: Development will be permitted where it makes effective and efficient use of land and buildings. Development proposals should: 1. Result in overall improvements to the built and natural environment; and 2. Be of a suitable scale for the site and not have a significant adverse impact on the character of the locality, the appearance of the street scene and the living conditions of neighbouring occupiers or future residents; and 3. Provide adequate off-street parking, access, covered and secure cycle storage which provides for the existing and proposed use; and 4. Not prejudice the potential for the comprehensive development of adjacent land; and 5. Provide outdoor amenity space and garden space at a level that reflects the character of the area and the scale of the development; and 6. Provide adequate, well designed, appropriately located and accessible bin storage areas; and 7. Be well-designed to create and support healthy living conditions.

hhsrsPolicy.description: GCC's published approach to HHSRS as stated in SRC-02

verbatimText.const: All HMO accommodation should be free from serious health and safety hazards and this is assessed using the housing health and safety rating system or HHSRS.

documentLocation.const: Section 3.0 – Health and Safety and the HHSRS

verbatimText.const: The system requires the assessment of 29 hazards grouped as: Damp and mould, excess cold / heat; Pollutants e.g. asbestos, carbon monoxide, lead; Overcrowding, security or lighting, or excessive noise; Poor hygiene, sanitation, water supply; Accidents – falls, electric shocks, fires, burns, scalds; Collisions, explosions, structural collapse.

documentLocation.const: Section 3.0 – Health and Safety and the HHSRS

verbatimText.const: Prior to an inspection the landlord/owner/manager of a property will be invited to visit the property with an officer from the Residential Services Team. This is to allow the landlord/manager to remedy any defects prior to enforcement action and to promote informal discussions.

documentLocation.const: Section 3.1 – How will the system be used in enforcement?

verbatimText.const: If informal action fails to remedy the situation the council is likely to move to formal action by serving an improvement notice on the owner, or agent as appropriate, requiring that hazards be reduced to a safe level within a set time. In more serious cases the council may serve a prohibition order prohibiting the use of all or part of a dwelling. Failure to comply with an enforcement notice is an offence and may lead to further legal action being taken.

documentLocation.const: Section 3.1 – How will the system be used in enforcement?

verbatimText.const: For minor hazards, the council may serve a hazard awareness notice informing the landlord of the hazards and identifying works to be undertaken to address these hazards. This type of notice is for information only.

documentLocation.const: Section 3.1 – How will the system be used in enforcement?

verbatimText.const: For more information please refer to the Councils Regulating Housing Standards Policy.

documentLocation.const: Section 3.1 – How will the system be used in enforcement?

fireSafetyPolicy.description: GCC's published fire safety standards and requirements for HMOs as stated in SRC-02

verbatimText.const: Fire Safety measures in HMOs are designed to alert tenants and occupiers to an occurrence of a fire and to prevent smoke and fire spreading to other parts of the property before residents have a chance to escape. In general, the following matters are considered when reviewing fire safety; Automatic fire detection systems. Protected routes for the occupiers to escape. Fire separation and compartmentalisation. Fire Doors. Emergency lighting. Fire fighting equipment. Fire safety compliant furniture and fittings. Fire risk assessments and property management.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.0 – Fire Safety

verbatimText.const: This information is generic in its content and its recommendations and each property must be assessed and judged individually as each property is unique.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.0 – Fire Safety

verbatimText.const: As a minimum in HMO's it is necessary to provide interlinked smoke detectors in the hallways and landings of the property and a heat detector in the kitchen and high risk rooms which are mains wired with battery back up. The detectors should be interlinked and comply with BS 5839:2019 part 6 - Grade D:LD2 type system. As the number of storeys in a HMO increases and the number of kitchens increases the level of fire safety detection will also increase.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.1 – Automatic fire detection

verbatimText.const: If you own or manage a property it is vital that you carry out an adequate fire safety risk assessment (FRA) and contact the Residential Services Team to discuss your current and proposed fire safety measures.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.1 – Automatic fire detection

verbatimText.const: All doors, walls, floors, and ceilings onto the escape route and the underneath and sides of all staircases on the route must be constructed to resist the passage and damage of fire, smoke and fumes for at least half an hour. Any electric or gas meters in the route must be enclosed in half-hour fire resisting construction and there must not be any storage or obstructions in the route. Any doors that open on the to means of escape and protected route including the final exit door from the property must be operable without a key.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.2 – Protected Route/Fire Separation

verbatimText.const: In a property with three or more storeys, all bedrooms, kitchens, and living room doors that open onto the protected route must be 30 minute fire doors and maintained in proper working order. Bathroom doors are only required to be a fire door if there is a fire hazard in the room i.e. a gas boiler.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

verbatimText.const: In properties with less than 3 storeys, a fire door is only required on high risk rooms which contain kitchen facilities.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

fireDoorRequirements.description: Verbatim fire door requirements as listed in Section 4.3 of SRC-02

default.verbatimText: Be half-hour (30 minutes) fire resisting (or one hour between commercial and residential accommodation).
default.documentLocation: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

default.verbatimText: Be operable from inside the room without the use of a key.
default.documentLocation: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

default.verbatimText: Be fitted with a self-closing device – which is regularly tested to ensure it fully closes the door.
default.documentLocation: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

default.verbatimText: Be fitted with an intumescent strip and cold smoke seal to the top and all side edges of the door or alternatively installed into all internal sides of the door frame.
default.documentLocation: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

default.verbatimText: Be fitted such that there is no more than a 3mm gap between the door and frame.
default.documentLocation: Section 4.3 – Fire Doors

verbatimText.const: Emergency lighting may be required in HMOs with three or more storeys and, in smaller properties where the layout and escape route is long or complex. The emergency lighting system should be mains wired with battery back up in the escape route designed to operate if the mains electrical supply fails. Emergency lighting will also need to be tested annually.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.4 – Emergency Lighting

verbatimText.const: All rooms containing cooking facilities should have a fire blanket. Suitably located on a wall near the cooking facilities and at a height of 1.2 -m 1.5 m from the floor. They must not be kept in a drawer or cupboard.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.5 – Fire Fighting Equipment

verbatimText.const: All upholstered furniture including chairs, sofas, mattresses, headboards, cushions and seat pads should comply with The Furniture and Furnishings (Fire Safety) Regulations 1988 as amended (2010).

documentLocation.const: Section 4.6 – Fire Safety Compliant Furniture and Furnishings

verbatimText.const: All fire doors, emergency lighting and fire detection equipment must be fitted and maintained by competent contractors in compliance with all relevant codes of practice and British Standards.

documentLocation.const: Section 4.7 – General

managementRegulationsPolicy.description: GCC's published duties under HMO Management Regulations as stated in SRC-02

verbatimText.const: Managers of HMOs are required to meet the requirements of the HMO Management Regulations which place specific duties on them. The manager means the person who has overall management responsibility for the premises which may be the landlord, HMO licence holder or their agent. Failure to comply with any of the Regulations is an offence and may lead to enforcement action being taken.

documentLocation.const: Section 5.0 – Management of Houses in Multiple Occupation (England) Regulations 2006 (as amended)

regulationDuties.description: Individual regulation duties as published verbatim in SRC-02

default.regulationTitle: Duty of manager to provide information to occupiers
default.verbatimText: The manager's name, address and telephone contact must be made available to each household and be clearly displayed in the HMO.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 3

default.regulationTitle: Duty of manager to take safety measures
default.verbatimText: The manager must ensure all means of escape from fire are free from obstruction, well maintained and signed. All fire alarms and fire fighting equipment must be maintained in good working order. The manager must ensure the design and structural condition of the property including roofs; balconies, windows and means of escape do not put the occupiers at risk of injury.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 4

default.regulationTitle: Duty of manager to maintain water supply and drainage
default.verbatimText: The manager must ensure that any tank, cistern or similar receptacle used for the storage of water for drinking or other domestic purpose is kept in a good, clean working condition and where necessary protected from frost. The manager must not unreasonably cause or permit the water or drainage supply that is used by any occupier of the HMO to be interrupted.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 5

default.regulationTitle: Duty of manager to maintain gas and electricity
default.verbatimText: Under the Gas Safety (Installation and Use) Regulations 1998 all gas appliances including cookers, heaters and boilers must have an annual safety inspection from a gas engineer registered with the Gas Safety Register. Any repairs identified must be completed without delay. Tenants should be shown a copy of the certificate. The manager must ensure that every fixed electric installation is inspected and tested at intervals not exceeding 5 years by a person qualified to undertake such inspection and obtain a certificate specifying the results of the test. On written request the manager must supply a current gas safety certificate issued by a Gas Safety Registered engineer to the local authority. The manager must not unreasonably cause the gas or electricity supply used by any occupier of the HMO to be interrupted.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 6

default.regulationTitle: Duty of manager to maintain common parts, fixtures, fittings and appliances
default.verbatimText: The manager must ensure all common parts of the HMO are in good and clean decorative repair, maintained in a safe working condition and kept free from obstruction. Particular attention should be paid to shared kitchens, bathrooms, WC's, handrails and banisters, stair coverings, windows and means of ventilation and light fittings. This duty also covers outdoor areas including gardens, outbuildings, yards, boundary walls and fences.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 7

default.regulationTitle: Duty of manager to maintain living accommodation
default.verbatimText: The manager must ensure that each unit of living accommodation within the HMO are maintained in good repair and fixtures, fittings and appliances provided as part of the tenancy are maintained in good repair and in clean working order.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 8

default.regulationTitle: Duty to provide waste disposal facilities
default.verbatimText: The manager must ensure that sufficient bins are provided for each household for the storage of refuse prior to collection. All tenants must be provided with the correct recycling and waste management information for the local authorities collections days and how to present waste for collection.
default.documentLocation: Section 5.0 – Reg 9

verbatimText.const: The management regulations also place a duty on tenants not to hinder managers in complying with these duties and to provide necessary information to help comply with these duties. To take reasonable care not to cause any damage to anything the manager has a duty to provide, to store and dispose of refuse appropriately and to comply with reasonable instructions regarding fire safety and means of escape.

documentLocation.const: Section 5.0 – Duties of the tenant

washingFacilitiesStandards.description: GCC's published washing facilities standards for HMOs as stated in SRC-02 Section 6.0

verbatimText.const: Where households are sharing a toilet and washing facilities the following must be provided: Up to five people may share a bathroom containing a toilet, wash hand basin and bath or shower. Up to six people may share these facilities if there is an additional WC and wash hand basin outside of the shared bathroom. If there are two bathrooms containing a toilet, wash hand basin and bath or shower then this is suitable for ten people to share. All baths, showers and wash hand basins must be equipped with taps providing a supply of cold and constant hot water. All bathrooms must be suitably and adequately ventilated. All bathrooms and toilets must be of an adequate size and layout. A wash hand basin must be provided in any rooms with a toilet and must be supplied with adequate supplies of hot and cold water.

documentLocation.const: Section 6.0 – Washing Facilities

spaceStandards.description: GCC's published space standards for HMOs as stated in SRC-02 Section 8.0

verbatimText.const: The number of persons permitted in each letting is determined by the useable space within the room and available communal space in the rest of the HMO. The whole space in a room may not be usable due to an unusual shape, low ceiling height or staircase bulkhead.

documentLocation.const: Section 8.0 – Space Standards

verbatimText.const: Rooms being used as sleeping accommodation with shared facilities by the occupiers must be minimum of: 6.5 square meters if occupied by one person, and 10.2 square meters if occupied by two persons. This size does not include any ensuite bath/shower rooms as this cannot be counted as bedroom floor area.

documentLocation.const: Section 8.0.1 – Bedrooms

verbatimText.const: If the room being used as sleeping accommodation also contains cooking facilities for the exclusive use occupier(s), rooms must be: 12.5 square meters if occupied by one person; 15.5 square meters if occupied by two persons. This size does not include any ensuite bath/shower rooms as this cannot be counted as bedroom floor area.

documentLocation.const: Section 8.0.1 – Bedrooms

verbatimText.const: Rooms located off kitchens where the only means of escape is through the kitchen are not suitable for being used as sleeping accommodation.

documentLocation.const: Section 8.0.1 – Bedrooms

verbatimText.const: Room Use / Number of Persons: Kitchen – 3 persons: 5m2, 4 persons: 6m2, 5 persons: 7m2, 6 persons: 8m2, 7 persons: 9m2, 8 persons: 10m2, 9 persons: 11m2, 10 persons: 12m2. Total communal living space – 3 persons: 16m2, 4 persons: 17m2, 5 persons: 18m2, 6 persons: 19m2, 7 persons: 20m2, 8 persons: 22m2, 9 persons: 24m2, 10 persons: 26m2.

documentLocation.const: Section 8.0.2 – Shared Kitchen and Communal Living/Dining room sizes

verbatimText.const: Discretion may be applied on the communal space that is available if the bedrooms are larger than the minimum sizes set out above.

documentLocation.const: Section 8.0.2 – Shared Kitchen and Communal Living/Dining room sizes

serviceStructure.description: The team structure and named roles responsible for HMO operational delivery as published by GCC

verbatimText.const: Gloucester City Councils Private Sector Housing service (PSH)

documentLocation.const: Regulating Housing Standards Policy – Responsibility of

verbatimText.const: OFFICER AUTHORISATION – PRIVATE SECTOR HOUSING – DELEGATED AUTHORITY – CORPORATE DIRECTOR

documentLocation.const: Appendix 2 – Delegated Powers of Authorisation – header

verbatimText.const: The person administering the Caution will be an appropriate Service Manager employed within Gloucester City Council.

documentLocation.const: Appendix 4 – Statement of Principles for Prosecutions and Simple Cautions – Simple Cautions

reason.const: GCC does not publish the size or composition of the Private Sector Housing team responsible for HMO licensing and enforcement. The number of officers, business support staff, or specialist roles is not stated on any published GCC page.

bestPracticeEvidence.const: North Northamptonshire Council (BP-NNC) publishes: 'our Private Sector Housing Operations team (made up of 5 Private Sector Housing officers and 1 Business Support officer)'. GCC publishes no equivalent information.

reason.const: GCC does not publish a dedicated contact channel (email address, phone number, or online form) for HMO licensing enquiries separate from the general 'heretohelp' contact. No named team email or direct line for the Private Sector Housing HMO team is published.

Multiple councils publish dedicated HMO team contact details distinct from general council contact numbers.
